serving.description: 1 set meal (approximately 1 English muffin + 1 fried egg + 1 sausage patty)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 180 g, comprising one toasted English muffin (approximately 57 g), one fried egg (approximately 50 g), and one sausage patty (approximately 73 g).

nutrient_source.title: Big Breakfast (Fried Eggs, English Muffin, Patty) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the composite dish 'Big Breakfast (Fried Eggs, English Muffin, Patty)', CR ID F08020004. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 where data is unavailable.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of English Muffin and analogous refined wheat bread products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite breakfast set meal. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being the English muffin (refined wheat flour). The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 55–77 for English muffins and white bread products; a midpoint estimate of 60 is applied. The sausage patty and fried egg contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially affect the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (46.1 g from 180 g portion) / 100 = 27.7, rounded to 27.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 463 mg/100 g with low potassium (140 mg/100 g), yielding an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that promotes fluid retention and elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal due to its high sodium load (463 mg/100 g; approximately 833 mg per 180 g serving), and should request no added salt during preparation, choose a lower-sodium patty, and add potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or tomato to partially offset the sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (25.62 g/100 g) from the English muffin with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), producing a carb-to-fibre ratio of approximately 14:1 that drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation; estimated GI of 60 and glycemic load of approximately 27.8 per serving are clinically significant.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this meal; if consumed, substitute the English muffin with a high-fibre wholegrain alternative, reduce portion size to no more than half a serving, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.2 g/100 g from the sausage patty and fried egg, combined with high sodium (463 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (13.72 mg/100 g), contributes to atherogenic and hypertensive cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this meal to occasional consumption; choose a lean chicken or turkey patty instead of pork or beef, use minimal oil for frying, and avoid adding cheese or butter to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red or processed meat patty is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) that amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; frying adds further pro-inflammatory lipid oxidation products.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the beef or pork patty with a chicken or fish patty, avoid frying in butter or margarine, and use extra-virgin olive oil if frying is necessary to reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Big Breakfast (Fried Egg, English Muffin, Sausage Patty) is a Western-style fast food breakfast set meal consisting of a toasted English muffin served with a pan-fried egg and a cooked sausage patty. The English muffin provides the primary carbohydrate base, contributing refined wheat starch that is the dominant driver of the meal's glycaemic response. The fried egg adds protein and fat, while the sausage patty — typically made from pork or beef — contributes additional protein, saturated fat, and sodium. The overall texture is a combination of soft, slightly chewy muffin, tender egg, and a firm, savoury patty. This meal is widely available at fast food chains and cafes across Singapore and internationally. Nutritionally, it provides 251 kcal per 100 g, with 12.22 g protein, 11.15 g fat, and 25.62 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 463 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The low dietary fibre content (1.8 g/100 g) relative to carbohydrate load raises the glycaemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat from the patty and egg contributes to cardiovascular risk. This meal is best consumed occasionally and can be improved by substituting the English muffin with a wholegrain alternative, choosing a leaner patty, and adding vegetables to improve fibre and micronutrient density.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact composite dish (CR ID F08020004) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from component analysis (egg and pork patty) using USDA FoodData Central as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on the English muffin as the dominant carbohydrate source, using the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g) is estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried egg and pork sausage patty, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this composite dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite breakfast set meal not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB for this composite dish and no exact composite match available in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on English muffin as the dominant carbohydrate contributor; University of Sydney GI Database reports GI 55–77 for English muffin and white bread products. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = 60 × 46.1 g carbohydrate / 100 = 27.7 (reported as 27.8). This dish is not a Singaporean-origin dish; multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. The dish is a Western fast food item widely available in Singapore but without a native Singaporean-language name.
